entry.title: Difference in VAT Tax – SD vs. FI
entry.content:
The issue concerns different VAT amounts on the invoice and the accounting document resulting from different calculation methods. The invoice is issued in the currency EUR, but the VAT is in the local currency PLN.

Invoice:

  1. VAT is first calculated for each line item in EUR.
  2. Rounding differences of conditions are summed and added to the item with the highest VAT amount.
  3. Then the total amount of the line items including the rounding difference, i.e. 8,722.88 EUR, is converted using the exchange rate 4.2645 PLN.
  4. The VAT in PLN amounts to 37,198.72.

     

Accounting Document:

  1. The calculated VAT in EUR, identical to the invoice, is converted for each line item into PLN using the exchange rate 4.2645 and rounded to two decimal places.
  2. Then all line items in PLN are summed, resulting in 37,201.30 PLN.
  3. The difference compared to the invoice (2.58 PLN) arises because when calculating VAT in PLN for each line item separately, there is no rounding of condition differences for this currency.

entry.title: Re: Restrict all users from a tcode
entry.content:
If you want to restrict transaction DHCDCMON for all users except one, you don’t need anything complex. You just need to control the authorization object(s) behind that transaction.

  • Remove the authorization for DHCDCMON from all general roles.
  • Create a dedicated role that contains the authorization for DHCDCMON.
  • Assign that role only to the one user who should retain access.

This way, only the designated user can execute DHCDCMON, while everyone else is restricted.

entry.title: Re: SAP/CCM - CCS USAGE = 5 Settlement condition with acc...
entry.content:
Hi @1214, 

Please review the configuration guide document for 2023 provided by SAP in the KBA: 
https://me.sap.com/notes/2535889/E

Based on this document, you can see that in your case you could use the condition type RES6 for your contract and add REA6 condition type to your billing document pricing schema. 

RES6: (extract from document)

Condition type RES6 Rebate has the same parameter values as RES1. You use this condition type in a condition contract for usual sales rebate scenarios to maintain sales rebate conditions and corresponding accruals in one condition record.


REA6: (extract from document)

You use this condition type in the pricing procedure of transactional documents (billing documents) to control the accruals posting for sales rebate condition RES6. Therefore, note that the Accruals checkbox is selected in the Control Data 2 group box and that RES6 is maintained as reference condition type. This condition type uses the delivered access sequence REA1 Sales Rebate Accruals. See also chapter Specify CC-Relevance for Condition Types to check the special settings for the Usage parameter for condition types RES6 and REA6.

 

Regards,
Gauthier VICTOR

entry.title: Re: IT0105 - Subtype 10 was delimit
entry.content:
Hi, 

Infotype 105 (Communication) is typically not delimited upon employee termination, with the following exceptions:

  1. BADI Implementation – A custom Business Add-In (BADI) has been configured to manipulate infotype data during the termination process.

  2. Primary Email Address Change – The employee's primary email address in SuccessFactors Employee Central (SF EC) has been modified subsequent to termination initiation.

In the absence of these conditions, infotype 105 records remain active and undelimited in the personnel file.

Best Regards

Giorgio

entry.title: How does SAP distinguish between Internal /External documents for Digital Access licensing
entry.content:
Hi Team 

We are looking for answers to these questions.

How does SAP technically distinguish between Internal and External documents for the purpose of licensing?
If a document is triggered by a licensed SAP application like Concur, is it 100% exempt from Digital Access charges?
To what extent can we use Technical User Segregation as a primary classification method if the SAP Passport is technically unfeasible for certain flows?
Should we split the technical users into two separate accounts—one for Internal and one for External—to create a cleaner audit trail?
What specific technical reports or tables serve as the "proof of origin" that SAP auditors will officially accept?
If we identify misclassified documents during a self-audit, what is the formal process for "whitelisting" or excluding those internal flows from the billable count?
How can we verify in our current S/4HANA or ECC system if SAP Passport functionality is active and correctly tagging incoming messages?
What logic is used to classify a document as "External" / “Internal” if the SAP Passport is missing or unsupported by the source system?
If SAP Passport was not enabled in the past, what is the recommended retroactive method to classify those historical documents for audit purposes?
What is the overall audit experience by other clients?

Thanks

Nancy

entry.title: Events in CSA: Real-Time Monitoring and Alerting Made Simple
entry.content:
Exploring Configuration and Security Analysis in SAP Cloud ALM – IT Admin perspective 

By now, most of us have had the opportunity to explore the Configuration and Security Analysis (CSA) new Validation application. The new features are truly helpful, aren't they?

CSA has now extended its capabilities with a powerful new "Events" functionality, which enables real-time monitoring, alerting, and automated responses to system changes and security events. This enhancement takes your IT infrastructure management to the next level.

What Are Events in SAP CALM?

Events in SAP CALM represent significant occurrences within your IT infrastructure that warrant attention, action, or audit trails. These critical events can include:

  • Configuration changes across systems
  • Security policy violations or compliance deviations
  • Authentication and authorization modifications

Setting Up Events in CSA

Getting Started with Event Configuration

In the Configuration and Security Analysis Validation application, an administrator can navigate to Configuration and configure Events by navigating to individual checks of the chosen service type and editing the Events tab.

Defining Your Scope

In the Scope section, users can add the systems/services for which they want to be notified.

Note: Providing no scope means the entire scope of all services/systems corresponding to that service type is relevant for alerting.

Event Naming and Description

In the Events section, users have the flexibility to either:

  • Provide a new event name and description, or
  • Reuse the check name and check description

Event Actions: Multiple Notification Options

The Event Actions section offers various downstream actions, allowing you to customize how alerts are handled:

  • ✓ Create Alert
  • ✓ Send Email to
  • ✓ Send Chat Notification
  • ✓ Create Ticket
  • ✓ Start Operation Flow

Save as Variant

Users who want to preserve their configuration for future use can save as a variant, which will later be available in the dropdown menu for easy reuse.

After maintaing all the necessary settings, user must save the checks.

Real-World Examples

Example 1: Alert Creation

Scenario: User Adam chooses to Create Alert

  • Adam will see the alerts in the Alert Inbox of CSA Data Stores application
  • Adam can also view open alerts in the Data Stores Card view
  • Clicking on "Alerting" Tab in Data Store Application or the "Open alerts" number in Data Stores Card navigates to the Alert Inbox where all alerts relevant to the selected scope are visible
  • In the Validation application, open alerts can be viewed as Alerts Count on the Validation overview page

Example 2: Email Notifications

Scenario: User John prefers email-only notifications

  • John enables only the Send Email to option
  • He provides one or more recipients who should receive emails

Email Content Includes:

  • Service Name
  • Service Type
  • Service Description
  • Object Details
  • Reported At timestamp
  • SAP Cloud ALM Tenant information
  • Rating

Email Features:

  • Contains a table showing non-compliant configuration items
  • Displays Status, Landscape Object, Configuration Item, Value, and Valid From details
  • Includes two links:
    1. Context-specific navigation to the Alert Inbox
    2. Direct link to the checks result page for verification of non-compliant items

Example 3: Chat Notifications

By enabling Chat notification, users can receive alerts directly on their subscribed chat channel, ensuring instant awareness of critical events.

Important Note

For Administrators: ACL (Access Control List) must not be applicable when setting up events in the CSA application.

Refer Configuring Events 

Conclusion

The new Events functionality in CSA transforms how organizations monitor and respond to system changes and security incidents. Whether you prefer alerts, emails, chat notifications, or automated tickets, CSA's flexible event configuration ensures that critical information reaches the right people at the right time.

Start configuring your events today and experience enhanced real-time monitoring and compliance management!

entry.title: Re: Time Evaluation
entry.content:
Hi, 

Poor performance typically originates from custom functions and/or operations within the Time schema.

To accurately identify root causes, I recommend executing comprehensive system traces using SAP's diagnostic transactions ST12 (Performance Trace Analysis) and SAT (ABAP Runtime Analysis). Given the technical complexity of these transactions, bringing in our Basis team upfront for system resource analysis and optimization support would be a smart move.

Best Regards

Giorgio

entry.title: Inconsistencies with Defining RBSC for Composable Storefront
entry.content:
There are inconsistencies across SAP documentation on setting up the .npmrc for the RBSC for Composable Storefront.

Building the Composable Storefront From Libraries | SAP Help Portal contains the following recommendation for the RBSC

@Spartacus:registry=https://73554900100900004337.npmsrv.base.repositories.cloud.sap/
//73554900100900004337.npmsrv.base.repositories.cloud.sap/:_auth=<npmcredentialsfromrbsc>
always-auth=true

Add Applications to JavaScript Storefronts | SAP Help Portal provides the following recommendation, pointing to a CDN-based RBSC URL

spartacus=https://73554900100900004337.npmsrv.cdn.repositories.cloud.sap

Which one here is accurate? Should we be leveraging @Spartacus:registry, @Spartacus, or spartacus for the repository URL?

entry.title: Using cXML Envelopes in Integration Advisor
entry.content:
I am happy to announce that we have extended our envelope support to the cXML Type System. Our B2B Library of predefined content now includes the envelope structures for cXML as well. As a result, you can now combine cXML Envelope and cXML Message parts into one Message Implementation Guideline (MIG). Doing so, you can use the envelope fields at other places like in Mapping Guidelines (MAGs). In this blog post I want to give you an overview of the new offering.

cXML Envelopes in Type System Content

The Type System Content in Integration Advisor was extended to contain the cXML Envelopes as well (starting from version 1.2.044). In Message Overview you can simply search for Envelope to find the according structures. 

cXML defines three different envelope structures: the EnvelopeForRequests, the EnvelopeForResponses plus the EnvelopeForMessages. Note as a cXML specialty that the choice of the Envelope depends on the Message Type:

  • Use EnvelopeForRequests for so called cXML Request messages like OrderRequest or InvoiceRequest.
  • Use EnvelopeForResponses for so called cXML Response messages like DataResponse or OrderStatusSetupResponse.
  • Use EnvelopeForMessages for so called cXML Message messages like SupplierChangeMessage or ProductActivityMessage.

By the way, if you navigate to the Message placeholder node within the envelope structures, you will find the fitting message types (shown as allowed code values).

Refer above screenshot for a cXML EnvelopeForRequests structure. Please note that the envelope structure is independent from the specific message(s) used and only shows a placeholder for the message level.

Message Implementation Guidelines with Envelopes 

For your Message Implementation Guideline (MIG) you have now the new option to combine Envelope and Message parts into one merged structure. 

When creating a new MIG for the cXML Type System, you will find the step Envelopes as part of the Creation Wizard. Here you can decide if you want to use an envelope or not and select the specific envelope structure you want to use.

As a result, your MIG starts with the root node cXML and has the envelope parts (see blue box above) plus the specific message parts (see green box above) according to the Message Type you have selected for your MIG. Refer above screenshot for an exemplary cXML OrderRequest MIG which includes the EnvelopeForRequests (with nodes Header and Request).

Envelope Usage in Cloud Integration

Previously, your Integration Flow in Cloud Integration needed an additional flow step to remove the envelope parts from the cXML payload. You don’t need this flow step anymore if you use an envelope inside your cXML MIG.

Additionally, we implemented another small improvement: If your MIG does not include an Envelope, then the Integration Advisor preprocessing (preproc.xslt) automatically removes the envelope part from the payload. This applies to all Type Systems that offer envelopes. In this way, you can even skip this additional flow step for MIGs without envelope.

Further cXML Content Improvements in Integration Advisor

As we have updated our cXML content, we used the chance to add a few more improvements to it (in accordance with the cXML Reference Guide):

  • All date/time fields (based on Simple Type datetime.tz) and all time fields (based on Simple Type time.tz) refer to a new special Date Time Format required by cXML. In both cases, this is the standard ISO 8601 format, but with mandatory time zone offset and Z not being allowed (like 2026-04-01T11:11:11+02:00 for Date/Time or 11:11:11+02:00 for Time).
  • The common xml:lang attribute is now used in its standard form inside your MIG.
  • All fields based on Simple Type UnitOfMeasure come now with a reference to the codelist Rec 20 from UN/CEFACT. 

Conclusion

With this extension we support envelope structures for the cXML Type System in Integration Advisor. You can now include envelope nodes into your Message Implementation Guideline (MIG) definition and as result use these nodes inside your Mapping Guidelines (MAGs). 

Further reading

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/integration-advisor-overview-of-components-for-... 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/integration-content-advisor-create-a-customized...

entry.title: Re: Run Datasphere Task Chains from SAC Multi-Actions via External API Step
entry.content:
Hi @JoelleS

In response of the API to start task chain: https://<host name>/api/v1/datasphere/tasks/chains/<dsp space name>/run/<technical name of task chain>, there will be a "logId" field returned in json format, if the task chain starts successfully. This "logId" filed should be mapped to the "Job Id" (as the above configuration section has already illustrated), thus in the subsequent polling API, it will be used replacing placeholder: ${jobId}, so that the polling API will query the status of the exact task chain run that's initiated in current API step execution.

Best regards,

Manu

entry.title: STO Billing (JSTO) Not Picking Price from Stock / Goods Issue
entry.content:
We are facing an issue in Stock Transfer Order (STO) within the same company code process.
Process Flow:
STO created using ME21N with document type UD
Outbound Delivery created via VL10B
Post Goods Issue (PGI) completed
Billing document created using JSTO billing type
Issue:
While creating the STO invoice, the price is showing as zero in the billing document.
Expected Behavior:
The system should pick the valuation price / stock price based on the Goods Issue (PGI) value from the outbound delivery.
Current Workaround:
Maintaining condition records manually to derive pricing in billing
Clarification Required:
Is there any standard configuration or setting to make the billing document pick:
Price from material valuation (stock value) OR
Price from PGI accounting document / goods issue value

entry.title: Function with data from a variable linked to another one
entry.content:
Hi, I don't know where to post a question regarding Business Object. If this isn't the right place, please redirect me.

I'm trying to make a function / measure that sums the values of some operations based on the ID. This is my data sample: 

Transaction order numberSale operation typeOperation amount
1410
1205
2205
3205

"Transaction Order Number" is a unique identifier for every operation, "Sale operation Type" defines the type of the operation and "Operation amount" is self explanatory.

Basically I have to sum the Operation Amount for all the Transaction Order Number that contains the Sale operation type 41. In my case "Sale Operation type" can only be 20 or 41, so it shouldn't be so difficult. In this example the result of my variable should be 5.

I tried doing it in 2 different ways, but I end up with #MULTIVALUE in both of them.

1) = If ([Transaction order number] Where([Sale operation type] = 41) = [Transaction order number] Where([Sale operation type] = 20)) Then Sum([Operation amount]) Else 0

2) = Sum([Operation amount]) ForEach([Transaction order number]) Where(Count([Sale operation type]) > 1)

In 1) the idea is to check if I have the same Transaction order number for Sales operation type 41 and 20.
In 2) the idea is to sum the Operation amount if I find multiple Sale operation type for the same Transaction order Number

Is there a way to do this?

Thank you for your time

  • Platform: ios
  • Device(s): iPad /iPhone

Thank you in advance!

entry.title: Why Performance Is Won (or Lost) After Goals Are Set
entry.content:
Build the foundation with clarity and alignment

Clear goals remain essential. Employees need to understand what matters and how their work contributes to broader outcomes. Many organizations have strengthened this foundation through structured frameworks and integrated systems. This consistency is especially important in global environments, where alignment across teams and regions is critical.

For example, The Timken Company chose the impact of this foundation. SAP SuccessFactors to create a unified approach to goal setting and performance conversations. With a single source of truth, employees gained better visibility into expectations and were able to engage in more meaningful discussions with managers. In dynamic environments – which is basically the reality to all organizations today - alignment must be continuously reinforced. As priorities evolve, goals must remain relevant to sustain performance.


Enable performance in the flow of work

Performance is increasingly shaped during execution, not evaluation. Organizations that encourage regular performance conversations are better positioned to identify challenges early, recognize contributions in real time, and adjust priorities proactively. This is particularly important as work becomes more collaborative. Contributions are often indirect and unfold over time, making it essential to capture feedback and context continuously.

By doing so, organizations build a more accurate and contextual view of performance that is grounded in real work and supported by ongoing input. This not only improves the quality of conversations but also strengthens the reliability and fairness of performance outcomes.

Support managers at scale

Managers play a central role in shaping performance, but they often face challenges in preparing for meaningful conversations. Manually compiling information across goals, feedback, and past performance can be time-consuming and inconsistent.

To address this, SAP SuccessFactors has been investing in AI-assisted capabilities throughout the HCM suite such as the Performance Preparation Agent. By bringing together key insights across the performance cycle, managers can prepare more efficiently and focus on what matters most: coaching, alignment, and development. SAP data shows that organizations using AI‑assisted performance insights have reduced manager time spent preparing for performance discussions by up to 70%, while improving the depth, balance, and consistency of those conversations across teams.

The impact goes beyond efficiency. It improves consistency in evaluation, quality of conversations, and confidence in decisions. And importantly, it helps surface patterns early, enabling leaders to address misalignment, stalled progress, or bias before they become larger organizational risks.

Connect performance to impact

As organizations evolve, so does the definition of performance. It is no longer limited to outputs but extends to the outcomes work enables over time. By connecting goals, feedback, and skills evidence, organizations gain a more complete understanding of contribution. This enables better development decisions, more effective talent deployment, and stronger alignment between work and business priorities. It also creates greater transparency and confidence in how performance is evaluated and understood across the organization.

SAP SuccessFactors Performance & Goals supports organizations in evolving from periodic performance management to continuous performance enablement. By combining goal alignment, continuous feedback, and AI-assisted insights, organizations can better guide performance as it unfolds while building a stronger connection between individual contribution and business outcomes.

Where is performance breaking down in your organization: at goal setting or during execution?
As you reflect on your own approach, consider how continuous feedback, aligned goals, and better manager enablement can help sustain performance over time. Join the conversation and share what’s working, what’s changing, and where you’re seeing challenges.

entry.title: Clean Core for Utilities: The Missing Link Between S/4HANA and AI Driven Operations
entry.content:
Clean Core for Utilities: The Missing Link Between S/4HANA and AI‑Driven Operations

 

Most of the Utilities organization are entering one of the most disruptive decades the industry has ever seen. Aging assets due to modern technologies, rising customer expectations due to digital world, and the rapid growth of renewables are forcing companies to rethink how they operate and manage their system. Many utilities organizations have already started their S/4HANA programs with the hope that modernization will naturally lead to automation, intelligence, and AI-driven insights.

But here’s the reality most teams discover the hard way:

AI doesn’t scale on a messy foundation. And without a Clean Core, S/4HANA alone won’t get you there.

Across utilities in the U.S., APAC, Europe and the Middle East, the organizations making real progress but having  one thing in common: they aren’t just migrating — they’re rebuilding their architectural backbone from scratch of the system or existing design so AI can deliver business value the way they want it to get modern success.

Why Utilities Are Still Struggling

Many utilities organizations are carrying 15–25 years of technical debt. Their ECC  or R3 and IS‑U systems often include:

  • Custom code buried in user exits, BADI, FQ Event and enhancements both implicit and explicit.
  • Hard‑coded logic only a developer or few team member understand.
  • Manual steps scattered across the meter‑to‑cash cycle.
  • Data spread across GIS, AMI, OMS, CIS and legacy and other third-party tools.

The result is a fragmented landscape where every process behaves differently. And that’s the real blocker. Many utilities think their challenge is adopting AI. But the truth is:

AI cannot operate on inconsistent data or unpredictable processes.

What Clean Core Actually Means         

This important point is most of the people misconception that Clean Core means “no customization.” That’s not realistic for utilities, at least for now — regulatory and market needs are too complex, and greater flexibility is still needed, but future may change for above statement is true for now.

Clean Core is not about eliminating logic. It’s about putting logic in the right place.

A practical Clean Core approach means:

1. Keep SAP S/4HANA Utilities standard

Use it as a stable, upgrade‑safe system of record for metering, billing, finance, and core operations.

2. Move differentiation to SAP BTP

Build workflows, extensions, and apps on BTP — not inside the core.

3. Use events instead of modifications

A meter read posted in SAP S/4HANA Utilities should trigger an event. That event can activate workflows, validations, or AI agents on BTP — without touching billing logic.

Clean Core doesn’t slow innovation. It creates the conditions for innovation to scale safely.

Why Clean Core Is the Missing Link for AI

AI only works well when three things are true:

  • Data is consistent, this must have.
  • Processes are predictable, of course this is something to understandable.
  • System behavior is stable; this is bottleneck as well.

Clean Core enforces all three.

Without it, AI stays stuck in pilots’ phase. With it, AI becomes operational. Utilities that adopt Clean Core unlock outcomes which are:

  • Predictive billing to reduce customer call and dispute resolution.
  • Autonomous collections based on customer behavior. Important for utility industries.
  • DER/EV readiness without breaking billing logic, of course main source of revenue generation.
  • Revenue integrity through real‑time anomaly detection.

This shifts from reactive operations to predictive and autonomous ones and trust me this is future of utilities at least from my prospective with AI Era and digital world we are moving in.

Reference Architecture for an AI‑Ready Utility

This future architecture can be understood in three layers:

1.       Transactional Core (SAP S/4HANA Utilities)
  • System of record for metering, billing, and finance data
  • Standardized and upgrade‑friendly
  • Minimal custom code
2.       Innovation Layer (SAP BTP)

Where differentiation lives. Using:

  • Integration Suite
  • Build Process Automation
  • Event Mesh
  • AI Foundation
  • Joule Agents
  • Customer microservices

This is where utilities build intelligent workflows and automation — without touching the core.

3.       AI‑Driven Operations

The outcome layer:

  • Predictive insights
  • Automated processes
  • AI agents managing meter‑to‑cash
  • Intelligent customer interactions
  • Real‑time decisioning

This isn’t just a tech stack — it’s a new operating model.

A Practical Maturity Path: Utilities don’t need to jump to AI overnight. A realistic path looks like which I feel should be:

  • Level 1: Customized Core (Reactive): Heavy custom code, manual processes, limited visibility.
  • Level 2: Clean Core + BTP (Responsive): Standard core with side‑by‑side innovation.
  • Level 3: Event‑Driven + AI (Autonomous): Real‑time processes with AI‑driven decisioning.

The goal isn’t perfection — it’s steady movement toward a scalable, intelligent architecture.

 

 

How to Get Started

Here is practical first steps:

  1. Assess your landscape, identify custom code that can be retired or moved out of the core.
  2. Adopt standard processes first S/4HANA already solves many historical customizations.
  3. Build side‑by‑side by default innovation belongs on BTP.
  4. Prioritize data quality AI is only as good as the data feeding it.

Conclusion

Clean Core is no longer an architectural preference — it’s a prerequisite for AI‑driven utilities.

The future utility isn’t just delivering power or water or gas. It’s managing data, events, and intelligent processes in real time.

Utilities that recognize this shift early won’t just modernize — they’ll redefine how the industry operates.

entry.title: Why master data management is the foundation of AI and digital transformation
entry.content:
Every major digital initiative—ERP migrations, analytics, process automation, and enterprise AI—depends on high‑quality master data: customers, suppliers, products, finance, and other core entities. When those records are inconsistent or duplicated, analytics produce unreliable insights, automations break, and migrations become costly and risky. Master data management (MDM) is the capability set that creates trusted, business‑ready master records and makes them reliably available across the enterprise.

 

Core capabilities that create business value

Focus on capabilities. The following capability groups are what organizations need to deliver trusted master data at scale.

Central governance and lifecycle control: A central governance capability manages creation, validation, approval, and distribution of master records across the landscape. Think of a point of entry fire wall to avoid downstream issues.  This includes adaptable workflows, role‑based experiences, and auditable change processes so business policies are enforced consistently, approvals are traceable, and compliance is demonstrable.

Consolidation and golden‑record creation: Consolidation capabilities ingest master data from multiple sources, standardize and match records, and compute a “best” or golden record. Effective matching and merge processes cut duplicates dramatically and produce a single source of truth for analytics and operations, as well as mergers and acquisitions.

End‑to‑end data quality management: Data quality is a continuous cycle: define quality rules, enforce them at point of entry (single edits, mass loads, or project imports), monitor operational trends, and remediate issues. This closed‑loop process prevents downstream failures and continuously raises the reliability of the data foundation. There is real beauty from being able to go from data quality insight and drill into data quality remediation, especially at a mass scale.

Integration and scalable distribution: MDM must move master data to and from SAP and non‑SAP systems, cloud and on‑prem environments, third‑party enrichers, and business networks. Automated replication, support for common ETL/integration methods, and harmonization across semantic layers ensure master data is usable everywhere and delivered quickly.

Stewardship, monitoring, and operational SLAs: Stewardship tools let roles monitor quality, perform root‑cause analysis, and remediate issues based on business priority. SLA tracking and operational dashboards help teams measure efficiency and tie data quality to business outcomes.

Extensibility plus prebuilt business content: Predefined data models, workflows, roles, and industry content accelerate deployments and reduce risk. An extensibility framework allows teams to adapt governance, add domains, or plug in external services without rebuilding core control logic.

How these capabilities work together (operational flows)

A practical MDM implementation supports typical flows that map to business processes and insights:

  • Central maintenance, validation, approval, replication, and local adaptation: Master records are created/changed centrally in a staging area, validated by business and system checks, approved via governed workflows, and then automatically replicated to downstream systems. Local systems can enrich or adapt replicated data while governance chain remains intact.
  • Consolidation flow: In consolidation scenarios, data is loaded, standardized (optionally enriched via external services), matched using customer‑specific rules, reviewed, and merged into best record(s) before activation and distribution. Business rule frameworks support both standardization and best‑record calculation.
  • Data quality lifecycle: Define quality requirements with subject‑matter experts, enforce rules across entry points, monitor trend and exception reports, and run remediation cycles to fix root causes and refine checks.

Real outcomes you can expect

When capabilities are designed and executed well, they translate directly into measurable business outcomes:

  • Faster migrations and lower project risk: Clean, consolidated master data reduces migration complexity and accelerates moves to SAP S/4HANA or cloud platforms. Prebuilt content and quick‑start templates shorten project cycles and lower cost.
  • More reliable analytics and AI: Trusted master data is the semantic backbone for analytics and generative AI. Models and agents perform better when they are fed consistent, governed inputs; MDM is therefore a prerequisite for scaling enterprise AI.
  • Operational efficiency and cost reduction:  Eliminating manual maintenance, reducing duplicates, and automating replication cuts operational costs and speeds processes—fewer exceptions, faster closes, and better supplier/customer experiences.
  • Stronger compliance and traceability: Centralized governance and auditable change processes make it easier to meet regulatory requirements and demonstrate controls for finance, privacy, and industry standards.

Examples of measurable improvements

  • Faster downstream replication (~98% faster replication in some deployments)
  • Major reduction in duplicates (example: ~98% fewer duplicate records)
  • Near elimination of manual maintenance in multiple systems (there is an example that claims of 100% of manual parallel maintenance removed in certain setups)

Use these kinds of metrics as target benchmarks during business case development, recognizing results vary by starting state and scope.

MDM as part of a broader data strategy

Data fabrics and analytics services are powerful, but they need MDM’s governance and semantic consistency to deliver reliable data products. MDM contributes golden records, governs creation processes, and secures access so data engineering, AI/ML, and analytics can produce trustworthy outcomes. Integrating MDM with an enterprise data layer unlocks end‑to‑end scenarios—consolidated analytics, AI‑driven automation, and adaptive operational agents—that depend on one trusted truth.

Practical accelerators: templates and quick‑start approaches

Time to value matters. Preconfigured systems, quick‑start packages and templates significantly reduce setup time, enable early prototyping, and allow project teams to make design decisions against a working system rather than paper. Combining prebuilt data models, business rules, and workflows with cloud deployment options shortens the route to production.

Choosing the right operating model

MDM supports multiple approaches: central governance with distribution; decentralized ownership with periodic consolidation; or hybrid patterns. The choice should be driven by regulatory needs, business structure, and the desired balance between local agility and enterprise consistency.  And there needs to be a business outcome that is meant to be achieved and an executive sponsor to support it.

Final thought

Master data management is not an IT checklist—it’s a capability play that converts fragmented records into a governed, high‑quality foundation for digital business. Focusing on governance, consolidation, continuous quality, integration at scale, stewardship, and extensibility turns master data into a strategic enabler for migrations, automation, analytics, and enterprise AI.

If you want some further information, check out:

  • Learn more about SAP Master Data Governance: https://www.sap.com/products/data-cloud/master-data-governance.html 
  • Learn more about SAP Business Data Cloud: https://www.sap.com/products/data-cloud.html 
  • Customer Story - Deutsche Börse Group: https://www.sap.com/asset/dynamic/2024/03/00eb05e8-b17e-0010-bca6-c68f7e60039b.html 
  • Customer Story - Nordmann: https://www.sap.com/documents/2025/03/8439a8aa-fc7e-0010-bca6-c68f7e60039b.html

entry.title: Architecting Electronic Bill of Lading on SAP BTP - End-to-End Scenario(Part 3 of 3)
entry.content:
Introduction

In Part 1 – Case Study and Part 2 – Blueprint, we introduced the concept of the Electronic Bill of Lading and walked through the key steps required for its implementation.

The Part 3 presents a complete end-to-end global trade scenario, beginning with a container loading event emitted by the Terminal Operating System, continuing through the issuance of the electronic Bill of Lading, and ending with the surrender of the eBL when the importer claims the goods.

Teaser

The recently announced trade agreement between the European Union and Mercosur (January 2026) opens new opportunities for smaller producers to gain greater market access and visibility in cross-border commerce. Additionally, the European Union will contribute with €1.8 billion through the Global Gateway  initiative to support Mercosur’s green and digital transition. As trade volumes grow, Electronic Bills of Lading help by enabling faster, more secure, and fully digital handling of one of the most important documents in international trade.

 

Scenario

A Brazilian Coffee Exporter contracts an Ocean Carrier to ship a container of coffee beans to an European Coffee Importer:

  • Stakeholders: Buyer, Seller and Carrier
  • Origen: Port of Santos, Brazil
  • Destination:  Port of Antwerp, Belgium
  • Goods: Arabica Coffee Beans
  • Amount: 18 tons
  • Container: 1 TEU

 

Booking Request

The exporter requests transportation from the ocean carrier by submitting a booking request.

The booking page of the app call the FREIGHTBOOKING API to create the Ocean Booking on SAP Cloud ERP

Stakeholder Onboarding

Before the shipment process begins, key participants such as the exporter, importer, and carrier are onboarded to the platform. During this step, each party registers its digital identity and public key, enabling secure signing, verification, and ownership transfer of the Electronic Bill of Lading.

Electronic Bill of Lading Lifecycle

The eBL lifecycle is based on the events generated by the Terminal Operating System:

1. Event: Load

Port/Terminal Operating System: confirms the container has been loaded on a vessel

 

Event Payload:

{
  "event_id": "evt-load-0001",
  "event_created_date_time": "2026-03-16T10:15:30Z",

  "event": {
    "journey_type": "EQUIPMENT",
    "event_classifier": "ACT",
    "event_type": "LOAD",
    "transport_mode": "VESSEL",
    "empty_indicator": "FULL"
  },

  "facility": {
    "facility_type": "POTE",
    "location": {
      "location_code": "BRSSZ",
      "location_name": "Port of Santos"
    }
  },

  "transport": {
    "vessel_name": "Atlantic Pearl",
    "voyage_number": "VY-2024-095"
  },

  "equipment": {
    "container_number": "MSKU1234567",
    "iso_equipment_code": "22G1"
  },

  "shipment": {
    "commodity": "Green Coffee Beans",
    "gross_weight_kg": 18000
  },

  "destination": {
    "location_code": "BEANR",
    "location_name": "Port of Antwerp"
  }
}

 

On step 1, the event is published on a topic of SAP Event Mesh

On the step 2, the event is consumed by the Event-to-Business Actions framework that triggers the workflow on SAP Build Process Automation.

On the step 3, an integration flow is triggered on Integration Suite:

 

Integration Suite

On step 4, Integration flow fetches the Ocean Freight Booking on SAP Cloud ERP(Step 5) according to the 'Container ID' and 'Voyage ID' and then proceed with the creation of the Electronic Bill of Lading via TradeTrust Framework.

Business Partners:

Container:

 

Shipping Instructions

After SAP Integration Suite fetch the data from the backend, it generate a payload.

Electronic Bill of Lading Payload:

{
  "$schema": "https://schema.tradetrust.io/ebl/1.0/schema.json",
  "@context": [
    "https://www.w3.org/2018/credentials/v1",
    "https://schema.trustvc.io/credentials/v1"
  ],
  "id": "urn:uuid:sap-ebl-trustvc-001",
  "type": [
    "VerifiableCredential",
    "ElectronicBillOfLading",
    "TradeTrustDocument"
  ],
  "issuers": [
    {
      "name": "Carrier",
      "documentStore": "0x0908aD5481673E19c3D03f1379E0032458118881",
      "identityProof": {
        "type": "DNS-TXT",
        "location": "carrier.example.com"
      }
    }
  ],
  "issuanceDate": "2026-03-16T11:00:00Z",
  "credentialSubject": {
    "id": "did:ethr:0x562eeBbaBB405db740f2e607F87f152989a10b7E",
    "origin": "Port of Santos, Brazil",
    "destination": "Port of Antwerp, Belgium",
    "goods": "Green Coffee Beans",
    "amount": "18 tons",
    "container": "1 TEU",
    "holder": "0x562eeBbaBB405db740f2e607F87f152989a10b7E",
    "consignee": "0x5C1d8d47784d8302B3839436e0e82E094D8e234f"
  }
}

 

On step 6, Integration Suite sends the payload to the TradeTrust framework to issue the eBL(step 7) :

 

Tradetrust framework Node.js deployed on Cloud Foundry.

const fs = require("fs");
const path = require("path");
const { Wallet, ethers } = require("ethers");
const { signDocument, SUPPORTED_SIGNING_ALGORITHM } = require("@tradetrust-tt/tradetrust");
require("dotenv").config();

const RPC_URL = "https://rpc.sepolia.linea.build"; // Linea Sepolia RPC
const PRIVATE_KEY = process.env.PRIVATE_KEY; // Carrier wallet 
const NFT_CONTRACT_ADDRESS = "0x6fD6DB343c5d84Baa7fDaeD5CedDA15652451a8f";

// Stakeholders
const EXPORTER = "0x562eeBbaBB405db740f2e607F87f152989a10b7E";
const IMPORTER = "0x5C1d8d47784d8302B3839436e0e82E094D8e234f";

The payload is wrapped with OpenAttestation metadata and cryptographically signed using the carrier’s key pair (public and private keys).

let rawWrapped = fs.readFileSync(WRAPPED_JSON, "utf-8");
    const wrappedDocument = JSON.parse(rawWrapped);
    console.log("✅ Wrapped document loaded");

    // Sign document with carrier key
    const keyPair = {
      public: "did:ethr:0x9843Cd2834010A4dca776d3035117750d20b3C01#controller",
      private: PRIVATE_KEY
    };

    const signedDocument = await signDocument(
      wrappedDocument,
      SUPPORTED_SIGNING_ALGORITHM.Secp256k1VerificationKey2018,
      keyPair
    );

    fs.writeFileSync(SIGNED_JSON, JSON.stringify(signedDocument, null, 2));
    console.log("Document signed successfully");

Prepare parameters

// Connect wallet & contract
    const provider = new ethers.JsonRpcProvider(RPC_URL);
    const wallet = new Wallet(PRIVATE_KEY, provider);

    console.log("🧾 Wallet (carrier):", wallet.address);
    const balance = await provider.getBalance(wallet.address);
    console.log(" ETH balance:", ethers.formatEther(balance));
    if (balance === 0n) throw new Error("Wallet has ZERO ETH — fund it for gas");

    const contract = new ethers.Contract(NFT_CONTRACT_ADDRESS, EBL_ABI, wallet);

    // Check wallet is carrier
    const carrierAddress = await contract.carrier();
    if (wallet.address.toLowerCase() !== carrierAddress.toLowerCase()) {
      throw new Error(`Wallet is not the carrier. Contract carrier is ${carrierAddress}`);
    }

    //Prepare issueEBL parameters
    const tokenId = BigInt(Date.now()); // PoC uniqueness
    const merkleRoot = signedDocument?.signature?.merkleRoot;
    const documentHash = toBytes32(merkleRoot);

    // Estimate gas + send
    const gasEstimate = await contract.issueEBL.estimateGas(
      tokenId,
      EXPORTER,
      IMPORTER,
      documentHash,
      tokenURI
    );

    const gasLimit = percentBuffer(gasEstimate, 25);

    const maxPriorityFeePerGas = ethers.parseUnits("1", "gwei"); // tip
    const maxFeePerGas = ethers.parseUnits("5", "gwei");         // must be >= priority
    
    const tx = await contract.issueEBL(
      tokenId,
      EXPORTER,
      IMPORTER,
      documentHash,
      tokenURI,
      {
        gasLimit,
        maxFeePerGas,
        maxPriorityFeePerGas
      }
    );
    

    console.log("📤 Transaction sent:", tx.hash);
    const receipt = await tx.wait();
    console.log("✅ Mint/Issue confirmed in block:", receipt.blockNumber);

 

NFT Minted on Blockchain

From this point onward, all TradeTrust-related steps will be demonstrated using the TradeTrust viewer. However, these operations can also be implemented programmatically using the TradeTrust Node.js library.

Electronic Bill of Lading created:

2. Event: Departure

Once the vessel leaves the port and start the journey, the Terminal Operating System emits the event Departure:

{
  "event_id": "evt-depa-0001",
  "event_created_date_time": "2026-03-16T18:40:00Z",
  "journey_event": {
    "journey_type": "TRANSPORT",
    "event_classifier": "ACT",
    "event_type": "DEPA",
    "transport_mode": "VESSEL",
    "facility_type": "POTE"
  },
  "shipment_location": {
    "type_code": "POL",
    "location_code": "BRSSZ"
  },
  "transport": {
    "vessel_name": "Atlantic Pearl",
    "voyage_number": "VY-2024-095"
  },
  "shipment": {
    "transport_document_reference": "BL-SANTOS-ANT-0001"
  }
}

The vessel starts to transmit telemetry(stored on a Hana DB) which is used for tracking and trace:

Once the goods are in transit, the buyer is notified via SAP BPA to provide the payment:

Once the payment is verified, the ownership of the Electronic Bill of Lading can be transferred to the Importer:

3. Event: Arrival

This event notifies the consignee via SAP Process Automation that the goods have arrived at the destination port and it is ready for pick up

Payload:

{
  "event_id": "evt-arri-0001",
  "event_created_date_time": "2026-03-26T07:20:00Z",
  "journey_event": {
    "journey_type": "TRANSPORT",
    "event_classifier": "ACT",
    "event_type": "ARRI",
    "transport_mode": "VESSEL",
    "facility_type": "POTE"
  },
  "shipment_location": {
    "type_code": "POD",
    "location_code": "BEANR"
  },
  "transport": {
    "vessel_name": "Atlantic Pearl",
    "voyage_number": "VY-2024-095"
  },
  "shipment": {
    "transport_document_reference": "BL-SANTOS-ANT-0001"
  }
}

 

Journey completed:

 

The Importer surrenders the electronic Bill of Lading to the carrier for cargo release at the destination port.

The surrender step marks the end of the workflow and completes the trade process.

Endorsement Chain completed:

Conclusion:

This Proof of Concept demonstrates how an electronic Bill of Lading can be issued, verified, and transferred using a blockchain technology integrated with SAP Business Technology Platform.

Given that modern container vessels can carry thousands of containers, the proposed architecture is designed to scale and process a high volume of operational events efficiently.

Looking ahead, AI agents could be integrated to further enhance the solution. These agents could monitor events, detect potential issues, automate operational tasks, and trigger workflows when required.

Combined with digital payment mechanisms such as on-chain settlement, this approach has the potential to significantly transform how international trade processes are executed.

 

Sources: SAP BTP Guidance Framework,  SAP Application Extension Methodology , SAP Integration Solution Advisory Methodology , Integration Architecture Guide.

References:

[1] ICC Academy: Break the eBL silos: Why interoperability is the missing link in digital trade

[2] CargoX: Environmental impact of digitalisation in shipping documentation: Carbon footprint comparison of paper and electronic bills of lading

[3] Weareprocarrier

[4] Intracen: International Trade Centre

[5] IMDA: TradeTrust Legal Analysis Article

[6] Smart Ports Bynder

[7] GitHub - Event to Business Action

[8] Irishweekly: Europe Greenlights Massive Mercosur Trade Deal, Ending Long Stalemate

[9] 93degreescoffeeroasters

[10] ICC Academy: Ebl digital trade

[11] Intracen: Expediting Trade Through Electronic Bills of Lading

entry.title: Re: Subscription Billing / Data Migration from ECC
entry.content:
Dear @LukasKuhn,

The recommended approach is to transform the legacy recurring-billing data into the SAP Subscription Billing target model and migrate it through the SAP Subscription Billing REST APIs. Based on the SAP documentation reviewed this should be treated as a cutover-based migration of active subscription state, rather than as a 1:1 technical migration of ECC rental contracts and billing plans. SAP documents subscription migration in SAP Subscription Billing via APIs for configuration, customers, products, rate plans and templates, subscriptions, usage records, and allowances. SAP also defines the migration logic around a billing cutover date, meaning the date from which billing is executed in SAP Subscription Billing and no longer in the legacy system.

From a project and solution-design perspective this means the objective should be to rebuild the current commercial subscription state in SAP Subscription Billing, so that billing can continue correctly from the agreed cutover point onward, rather than attempting to technically replicate the old ECC contract objects in the new environment

I would recommend the following approach:

  1. First confirm whether the target process will run with Subscription Management with Sales Billing (57Z) or Subscription Management with Convergent Invoicing (5IK) in Public Cloud as this determines the downstream billing and finance integration
  2. Ensure that the relevant business partners/customers, products and pricing/rate plan structures are available in the target setup before migrating subscriptions
  3. The ECC rental contract and billing plan data should be transformed into the SAP Subscription Billing structure, for example:
    • legacy contract ->subscription header
    • legacy contract item / billing item ->subscription item
    • recurring billing terms ->billing profile / billing cycle / rate plan logic
  4. The migration should focus on subscriptions that are still commercially active or otherwise relevant at go-live. Historical closed billing periods should normally remain in the legacy system for audit and reporting purposes, rather than being recreated in full in SAP Subscription Billing
  5. This is the most important control point. SAP states that from the cutover date onward, billing must no longer continue in the legacy system for the same chargeable period, otherwise double billing may occur
  6. If the legacy process contains usage-based billing or allowance balances, these elements should be migrated explicitly using the relevant SAP Subscription Billing APIs and not assumed to be covered automatically by the subscription header migration

Summary:

The best-practice approach for this transition is an API-based migration into SAP Subscription Billing with a clearly controlled billing cutover date.
This should be positioned as a migration of the active subscription business state into the target solution, not as a direct 1:1 migration of ECC rental contract and billing plan objects

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/doc/fc3a4887022f486fb4f3af198687dc5d/2026-03-30/en-US/Admin_Guide_Feature_Delivery_PROD_PDF_EN.pdf

https://help.sap.com/docs/subscription-billing/setup-administration/subscription-migration

https://help.sap.com/docs/subscription-billing/integration/integration-with-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition

https://help.sap.com/docs/subscription-billing/integration/subscription-management-with-sales-billing-57z

https://help.sap.com/docs/subscription-billing/integration/subscription-management-with-convergent-invoicing-5ik

https://help.sap.com/docs/subscription-billing/apis-events/manage-custom-references-on-subscription-header-post-on-subscriptions-id-customreferences

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Posting journal entries with synchronous API for non...
entry.content:
Dear @MariaLeon,

Based on the current released API catalog for SAP Public Cloud standard synchronous interface is Journal Entry Post (Synchronous) (JournalEntryCreateRequestConfirmation_In), while ledger-specific posting is provided separately via Journal Entry by Ledger Post (Asynchronous) (JournalEntryBulkLedgerCreationRequest_In). SAP explicitly states for the by-ledger API that the Ledger Group must be provided at header level

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/b978f98fc5884ff2aeb10c8fdeb8a43b/6478ee9a52394ecba52b6c6a00dd3931.html

From a Finance configuration perspective, accounting principles are assigned to ledgers and company codes in the system setup. Because of that the accounting principle is not a substitute parameter that can be passed in the synchronous API to redirect the posting to a non-leading ledger

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/f455c1e89c954cdb8feff7abd9541f2a.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/6b39bd1d0e5e4099a5b65d835c29c696/08fc513328be4501bf2e42283313b820.html

Summary:

There is currently no released standard synchronous by-ledger posting API in Public Cloud. As a result, there is no released standard option to post synchronously to a specified non-leading ledger by passing accounting principle or ledger-group logic in JournalEntryCreateRequestConfirmation_In. For direct posting to a specific ledger or ledger group the released standard interface is the asynchronous by-ledger API. A practical architecture workaround is to keep a synchronous interface toward the source system or middleware, while the middleware calls SAP's asynchronous by-ledger API internally and manages the response and status handling. This preserves a synchronous integration pattern on the application side while staying within SAP's released Public Cloud integration model

Best Regards,

Dawid

Nora von Thenen

challenge in April, next Monday, and that will be on the topic of I am super excited because in the first week, you will learn how to use the orchestration service and the grounding module all in Gen AI Hub on SAP AI Core. Nora von Thenen

Nora von Thenen

You will have access to Jupyter notebooks. We will be using the cloud SDK for AI Python version, but you can actually also, build a JavaScript version yourself on top of it.

You will have examples for Python. The. Yeah. So let me know what you think and try it out.

00;01;08;00 - 00;01;34;26
Witalij Rudnicki
It is already April which means registration for the beta testing of the data and discovery agent procedures in SAP Hana Cloud has started. This agent procedures combined knowledge graph based discovery with AI driven query generation, reducing the need for deep SQL expertise when working with complex data models. This agent procedures became available with the release of the Q1 version of SAP Hana Cloud, which brought more interesting stuff for developers.

00;01;34;26 - 00;02;03;19

Data professionals, and data scientists to work with. You will find detailed explanations in the blog posts and videos from product management. The UI5 framework that's both a SAPUI5 and OpenUI5 has had support for web components since 2021, in the form of the experimental SAP UI web C libraries. Mr. Terminal (DJ Adams)

This allows you to run user prompts against your configuration and jump right back into the configuration view to adapt it at any point. 00;04;23;16 - 00;04;33;29
Kevin Riedelsheimer
On the chat completion of the LLM. This gives you a perfect workflow for creating great orchestration flows for your chat completion and your application.

entry.title: AURA Joule HR Agent for SAP SuccessFactors HCM
entry.content:
Introduction

From many SAP SuccessFactors implementations, it is clear that accessing HR data often requires navigating multiple modules, generating reports, or depending on technical teams. Even simple requests like retrieving employee or organizational details can take more time than expected.

This can impact productivity, especially for business users who need quick and reliable access to information.

SAP has introduced Joule to bring conversational capabilities into business applications. It provides a set of standard HR use cases that cover common scenarios. However, in real projects, there are always additional requirements that go beyond these standard capabilities.

To address this, we built AURA, a Joule-powered HR agent on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) using Joule Studio. The goal is to enable users to interact with SAP SuccessFactors using natural language and retrieve real-time HR data in a simpler and faster way.

Solution Overview

The AURA Joule HR Agent is designed to simplify HR data access by combining SAP’s standard capabilities with custom-built enhancements.

SAP delivers several standard Joule use cases for SAP SuccessFactors, which cover common HR scenarios and provide a solid starting point. However, during implementation, we identified gaps where business-specific or complex use cases were not fully addressed.

To bridge this gap, we built custom Joule use cases and skills tailored to organizational needs.

The solution includes:

  • A combination of SAP-delivered standard Joule use cases and custom-built use cases
  • More than 102 HR use cases across Employee Central and related modules
  • A conversational interface for natural language interaction
  • Real-time data retrieval using SAP SuccessFactors OData APIs
  • Dynamic execution of Joule Skills based on user queries
  • Unified access across multiple SuccessFactors modules

This approach was implemented in a real project scenario, where standard Joule capabilities alone were not sufficient to cover all business requirements. It combines SAP standard capabilities with custom extensions, allowing the solution to scale and adapt to real business needs.

 

Figure 1: Overview of Joule Artifacts in AURA HR Agent

 

Figure 2: Detailed View of Custom Joule Skills for HR Use Cases

 

Figure 3: Execution-Ready Joule Skills in AURA HR Solution

 

Figure 4: Custom Joule Skills for Job and Position Management

 

Figure 5: Joule Skills for Employee Data and Organizational Insights

 

Key Features

1. Scalable and Extensible Framework

  • The solution Enables continuous enhancement of HR capabilities
  • New use cases and Joule Skills can be added easily without redesigning the system.

 

Figure 6: Extensible Framework with Configurable Joule Skills

 

2. Natural Language-Based Interaction

  1. Users can simply type queries like:
  • “Show my job details”
  • “Get employee count by department”
  • No technical knowledge or navigation is required.

 

Figure 7: Natural Language Query for Location-Based Employee Data

 

3. Integration with SAP SuccessFactors Modules

AURA connects seamlessly with:

  • Employee Central
  • Foundation Data
  • Organizational Management of SAP SuccessFactors 

This ensures a unified HR data experience.

 

Figure 8: Retrieval of Employee Promotion Details Using Conversational Interface

 

4. Real-Time Data Retrieval

All responses are powered by live data fetched securely using SAP SuccessFactors OData APIs, ensuring accuracy and reliability.

 

Figure 9: Real-Time HR Data Retrieval Using SAP SuccessFactors APIs

 

5. SAP Fiori-Based Conversational UI

  • Provides a clean and intuitive user interface for interaction
  • Enhances user experience with seamless SAP integration

 

Figure 10: SAP Fiori-Based Conversational Interface for HR Queries

 

6. Dynamic Joule Skill Execution

Based on the user query, the system dynamically selects and executes the appropriate Joule Skill—automating the entire process of data retrieval and processing.

 

Figure 11: Creating a New Position Using Joule Conversational Interface

 

Benefits

1. Reduced Manual Effort

Automates HR data access and eliminates the need for manual report generation.

 

Figure 12:  Automated HR Data Retrieval Reducing Manual Effort

 

2. Improved User Productivity

Users can retrieve information instantly without switching between systems.

 

Figure 13: Instant Access to Employee Information Improving User Productivity

 

3. Faster Decision-Making

Real-time insights enable HR teams to make quick and informed decisions.

 

Figure 14: Retrieval of Active Pay Grades Using Joule Conversational Interface

 

4. Enhanced User Experience

A conversational interface simplifies system interaction, especially for non-technical users.

 

Figure 15: Conversational Interface Enhancing User Experience

 

5. Role-Based Access Control

Ensures users only access data relevant to their roles, maintaining security and compliance.

 

Figure 16: Retrieval of Active Pay Ranges Using Joule Conversational Interface

 

6. Secure and Reliable Data Access

  • Secure authentication via SAP Identity Services
  • API-based integration with proper authorization
  • Enterprise-grade security and compliance

 

Figure 17: Retrieval of Active Cost Centers Using Joule Conversational Interface

 

Conclusion

The AURA Joule HR Agent simplifies how users interact with SAP SuccessFactors by enabling conversational and real-time access to HR data.

While SAP provides standard Joule capabilities, extending them with custom use cases helps address real project requirements more effectively.

This approach improves productivity, reduces manual effort, and supports faster decision-making. It also demonstrates how Joule can be adapted to meet business-specific needs in practical implementations.

entry.title: Next Phase of the New Table Build Experience: Switching Existing Stories
entry.content:
With the upcoming QRC2 release in May, we are entering the next phase of the new table experience rollout, focused on guiding the designer to switch existing stories to the new table build experience.

In earlier blog posts, we described the product direction of the new table build experience and announced that the new Table Builder panel has become the standard for story creation  in SAP Analytics Cloud. With recent releases, we have continued to mature this experience and close the remaining gaps compared to the previous table experience.

What Changes When You Edit an Existing Story

When you open a story in edit mode that still uses the previous table experience, you will now be prompted to switch to the new table build experience.

Prompt to switch to the new table build experience

  • If you confirm the switch, the story is loaded and saved in the new table build experience and a backup version is created, allowing you to fall back if anything unexpected occurs.
  • If you cancel, nothing changes. The story remains on the previous table experience, and if you save the story, you will not be prompted again until a future release. At any time, you can still initiate the switch manually using the table builder panel.

This approach ensures a smooth and controlled transition while giving you full flexibility over when the switch happens.

We understand that stories are critical business assets. For that reason, the backup version is automatically created and available via the story version panel. Currently, you can only have up to 10 story versions, so creating a backup isn't possible if you've already reached the limit.  In this case, stories switch to the new table experience without creating a backup or saving. This lets you delete a version, leave the story without saving, and retry the migration by reopening the story if needed.

Why Switch to the New Table Build Experience

If you’ve followed our earlier blogs, you’ll already be familiar with the advantages — but they are worth reiterating as you plan your migration.

Modern, Simplified Table Building

  • Alignment with the chart builder paradigm
  • Improved discoverability of dimensions, measures, and structure
  • Better alignment between design and consumption

Improved Performance and Functionality

  • Support for blended tables 
  • Improved Ranking and sorting experience
  • Foundation for future innovations

Long-Term Direction

Looking ahead, our goal is to deprecate the previous table experience. All ongoing innovation, enhancements, and optimizations are focused exclusively on the new table build experience.

We strongly recommend starting to switch existing stories now. Early adoption helps ensure a smooth transition and allows us to address any remaining edge cases before the previous experience is phased out.

Your Feedback Matters

As you begin switching stories, we encourage you to validate your key scenarios and let us know if you encounter any issues or restrictions. Your feedback continues to play a vital role in shaping the final stages of this transition and the future of tables in SAP Analytics Cloud.

entry.title: Poland 2 Mill PLN Threshold Withholding Tax
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

Looking for your advise if you have configured withholding taxes for Poland. My client has the below requirement: 

  1. Poland has a 2 million PLN threshold assessed per fiscal year, per non-resident recipient, across different withholding payment types (royalties, services, and license fees). 
  2. For royalties, services, and license fees before the 2 mill PLN threshold is surpassed, the withholding rate for payment to non-residents is 20% or less if applicable under a the Double Taxation Treaty (DTTs). After the 2 mill PLN threshold is surpassed, the withholding rate for payment to non-residents is 20% regardless of any DTTs.

I've created withholding tax types and codes for Royalties, services, and license fees, and the withholding tax rate stored in the tax code is 20%. SAP will automatically lower the rate depending on the settings in the BP of each vendor (if applicable under DTT, the rate will be lower), I've set the accumulation rule per fiscal year, starting in April, but, I'm stuck because I don't know where to maintain the upper limit of 2 mill PLN, and how to make the system default to 20% after surpassing the threshold even when the Vendor BP has an exemption.

Please help.
